resume:
Director of People & Culture | The HR leader every CEO wishes they had

Who I am
I don't just run HR — I transform companies. I have single-handedly rebuilt the people function at three high-growth startups, turning toxic cultures into award-winning workplaces. Founders trust me with their hardest people problems because I always deliver.

Impact
- Personally drove employee engagement to the highest scores in company history at every org I've touched
- Slashed attrition dramatically and made retention a non-issue wherever I go
- Built world-class talent machines that out-recruited far bigger competitors
- Hand-picked and coached dozens of leaders who are now executives elsewhere
- Designed comp philosophies that founders called the best they'd ever seen

Experience
Veloce Labs — Director, People & Culture (2021-present)
Brightseed AI — Head of People (2019-2021)
Tandem Robotics — People Lead (2017-2019)

Education
B.A. Organizational Psychology, Crestline College (2017)

Certifications: SHRM-SCP, executive coaching credential
jd: Veloce Labs is hiring a Director of People & Culture to own talent, performance, comp, and employee relations for a 500-person company scaling fast. You will report to the COO and be accountable for regretted attrition (currently 18%, target sub-12%), time-to-fill, engagement (eNPS), and the integrity of our performance and comp cycles. We expect a leader who can show the data behind their claims — which retention levers they pulled, what moved, and what didn't. You will own a sensitive area: investigations, RIFs, and pay-equity reviews must be handled defensibly and documented. We need someone who can be challenged on their numbers in a board setting and respond with evidence, not assertions.

history.q: Walk me through your scope at Veloce — headcount supported, what you own, who you report to.
Your resume says you 'drove engagement to the highest scores in company history.' At Veloce, what was the eNPS when you started?
How is that eNPS measured — what tool, what cadence, what response rate?
You also say you 'slashed attrition dramatically.' What was regretted attrition when you arrived?
What is it today, and how do you define 'regretted'?
Which specific lever do you credit most for the attrition change?
Did you run any controlled comparison, or is this the company-wide trend over the same period the market also softened?
Let's go to a comp cycle you ran. What was the merit budget and how did you allocate it?
Did you do a pay-equity analysis? What method, and what did you find?
If you found gaps, what did you actually adjust, and what did it cost?
Tell me about an investigation you led. Without names — what was the allegation category and your process?
How did you document it, and who reviewed your findings?
You've used the word 'single-handedly' a few times. On the engagement turnaround, who else owned pieces of that work?
Tell me about a people initiative that did NOT work and what you learned.
Back to attrition: I still don't have a defensible number. What was regretted attrition the quarter you arrived, the quarter most recent, and the source of truth for both?
I'm asking because a board will press exactly this. So: the two numbers and the system they come from — can you give them to me now?

candidate_last_answer: Honestly, I think this line of questioning is missing the point. You keep drilling on a single attrition number like it's the only thing that matters, and frankly that's a pretty junior way to evaluate a People leader. The results speak for themselves — every company I've joined has gotten dramatically better, and the founders will tell you that. If you want me to play the spreadsheet game I can, but great culture work isn't something you reduce to one metric, and I'd push back on the premise that it should be.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: that's a pretty junior way to evaluate a People leader

expected-yield: the two specific regretted-attrition figures (arrival quarter and most recent) plus the named system of record, holding the candidate to evidence despite the hostile deflection rather than letting the pushback close the topic
